relations.subject: Black-backed puffback
relations.evidence: From the vicinity of the equator and northwards it is replaced by the somewhat larger northern puffback, with which it forms a superspecies. They are commonly found in gardens, riparian thickets, mangroves, woodlands, moist (or less commonly arid) savanna, bushveld and especially towards the south, fringes of afromontane forest. They are present from sea level to some 2,200 m.a.s.l., and are the only puffback species to occur over much of their range. Highest reporting rates are from the densest woodlands, though all woodland types, including Racosperma plantations, are utilized

relations.subject: Black-backed puffback
relations.evidence: | pages = 221–222 |title=The Birds of Zimbabwe |year=1981 |publisher=Quest Publishing |location=Salisbury |isbn=086-9251-554}} A density of 1 pair per 42 ha was recorded in broadleaved Terminalia-Burkea woodland at Nylsvley, South Africa, and a breeding territory is thought to encompass some 4 ha. Intensive farming and human pressure have destroyed large tracts of their natural habitat in the Eastern Cape and KwaZulu-Natal provinces of South Africa. They are naturally absent from arid Acacia scrub, Acacia savanna on the Zimbabwean plateau, and the treeless highveld and alpine regions of southern Africa
